item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy greatly the concept of a "forbidden phone", but having it play out as a fairly vanilla memetic infection/selective memetic kill agent really hurts it. It's not the worse and some of the ideas with which it toys show promise, but those make up the minority of the article.

Sorry, this is ultimately a -1 from me, though barely so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Security, put Mr. N████ here in a regular quiet box.

Shouldn't it state that Dr. P said this out loud?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you! I was aiming for more a World of Babel scenario teaser.

I'm in the planning stages for a tale about an interaction with this tribe. My idea is that some of the MTF members will go and investigate.
I'm thinking of calling them the "Stuffed-Nose People" - the pharyngeal nasal would be their only nasal, so if auditory infohazards are blocked out it sounds like they would have no nasals, like someone with a bad cold

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I disagree with most of the criticism here. This article teases the beginning of a World of Babel or Deific Subjugation (depending on how you read the addendum) scenario that is torturing my curiosity

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was really enjoying this until the addenda. The interview isn't terrible, but it really doesn't convey enough, and the characters aren't interesting enough on their own to sustain the story. Likewise, the final addendum didn't feel like a good place to end - there isn't a lot of payoff to be had.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
HMM

On the one hand, this is right up my alley and I'm pretty sure I can make that sound? (I'm never quite sure where the delineation of all the rear-throat areas are.)

On the other, it doesn't go anywhere, really. You tease us with that immune tribe in the Amazon, which is something I have definitely seen before. The interview doesn't really come to any conclusions save "the Foundation doesn't know everything". The redaction in the list of symptoms is kind of cheesy.

I like this, but I want to like it more. The SCP wiki has become a rather exacting place to write for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I appreciate the suggestion. I'm thinking of expanding the tribe into an (as yet unwritten) tale. When it's done I'll link it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this was an interesting turn from the amount of mathematical skips. Idea is solid enough, though I would love some expansion on the tribe, because now the Addendum about it doesn't add much.
+1

item.title: First SCP!
item.content:encoded:
I'm a long time reader, first time poster! Thank you to the great and powerful Leveritas for his readthrough.

I'll be the first to admit this is an old-style skip, without the larger universal implications. I hope it's still enjoyable!

+ Ideas for expansion
- Hope you enjoy!
  • I personally think it's about time there's an MTF for linguistic-related SCPs, and I'm proud of the name choice1.
  • Regarding the Amazon tribe which uses the language, I'm thinking of expanding that into an as-yet-unwritten tale. Maybe there could be a Prescriptivists series. There are plenty of great linguistic SCPs that would work with it!
Footnotes
1. Prescriptivists are people who claim that language should follow set rules, and that other forms ought to be suppressed. Generally associated with grammar nuts/English teachers. It's not usually considered a good thing in linguistic scholarship, but when a language could destroy human cognition, it seems like a good idea.
